does: This paper introduces VLFeedback, a large-scale dataset of over 82K multimodal instructions with AI-generated feedback and rationales designed to align vision-language models without human annotation. The authors train Silkie, an LVLM fine-tuned using direct preference optimization on VLFeedback, and demonstrate its effectiveness across multiple evaluation dimensions. Silkie achieves 6.9% improvement on perception tasks and 9.5% on cognition tasks compared to its base model, reduces hallucination on MMHal-Bench, and shows improved robustness against adversarial attacks. The core contribution is establishing that AI feedback at scale can effectively supervise LVLM alignment, addressing the bottleneck of expensive human annotation.
connects: This work builds on the emerging paradigm of using AI feedback for model alignment, extending recent approaches like those in language model feedback datasets to the multimodal domain. It responds to the practical challenge of scaling supervision for increasingly capable vision-language models, building on foundations in preference optimization methods such as DPO and extending principles from vision-language model evaluation. The paper connects to prior work on LVLM hallucination mitigation, safety alignment through preference learning, and the broader research trajectory toward reducing human annotation costs in model training through self-generated or model-generated supervision signals.
enables: This dataset and approach enable future research into efficient LVLM alignment without human bottlenecks, allowing rapid iteration as vision-language models continue evolving. The work points toward automated quality assessment pipelines for multimodal model outputs and systematic understanding of what constitutes effective AI feedback for vision-language tasks. It opens directions for investigating when AI feedback is sufficient versus when human feedback remains necessary, scaling alignment to increasingly capable and specialized vision-language models, and developing better methods for generating high-quality multimodal rationales. The reduced reliance on human annotation could accelerate deployment of safer, more reliable vision-language systems in real-world applications.
